name: FAST
tagline: Say what you need while keeping your self-respect intact.
situations: Tempted to apologize for having a need
About to cave just to keep the peace
Wanting to act in line with your values under pressure
summary: FAST is DBT's skill for protecting your self-respect in an interaction: be Fair, no excessive Apologies, Stick to your values, and be Truthful. Where DEAR MAN gets the objective and GIVE protects the relationship, FAST protects your relationship with yourself. It's the antidote to walking away feeling like you sold yourself out.
howItWorks: People often win an argument or keep the peace at the cost of their own integrity — over-apologizing, abandoning a value, or bending the truth to be liked. FAST keeps self-respect in the equation: fairness to both yourself and the other person, dropping the reflexive apologies that signal you're in the wrong for existing, holding the line on what matters to you, and staying honest rather than exaggerating or playing helpless. You leave the conversation still respecting yourself.

steps.instruction: Fair: be fair to yourself and the other person — don't sacrifice your needs, and don't steamroll theirs.
No (excessive) Apologies: drop the reflexive 'sorry' for having a need or an opinion.
steps.note: Apologize for real wrongs, not for taking up space.

steps.instruction: Stick to your values: don't abandon what matters to you just to be liked or to win.
steps.mantra: I can be kind and still keep my values.

steps.instruction: Truthful: don't lie, exaggerate, or act helpless to get your way.

whenToUse: When you tend to cave to keep the peace
When you over-apologize for having needs
Any time self-respect is on the line in a conversation
whenNotToUse: As rigidity that refuses all compromise — fairness cuts both ways
When a genuine apology is actually owed
With someone unsafe, where protecting yourself outranks the conversation
tips: Notice reflexive apologies and cut them
Decide your non-negotiable value before the conversation
Pair with DEAR MAN (objective) and GIVE (relationship) for the full set
Self-respect is the goal, not winning

evidence.summary: FAST is a DBT interpersonal-effectiveness skill; DBT has strong meta-analytic and Cochrane-level support, and assertiveness training is well validated.
citations: Linehan, M.M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual, 2nd ed. Guilford Press.
